title: Your Microbiome
summary: How feeding choices shape the trillions of microbes living in your gut.

questions.prompt: Bifidobacterium and many gut helpers grow best when you eat…
choices: Plenty of fiber from fruits, vegetables, and whole grains
Nothing at all for several days
questions.explanation: Fiber feeds beneficial gut microbes — they ferment it into short-chain fatty acids the body uses for energy.

questions.prompt: Akkermansia muciniphila lives along the gut wall and usually helps by…
choices: Feeding on the mucus layer and helping keep the gut lining strong
questions.explanation: Akkermansia feeds on the gut's mucus layer — its presence is often linked to a stronger intestinal lining.

questions.prompt: An antibiotic can disturb the microbiome because it…
choices: Reduces many bacteria at once, including helpful ones
Adds extra microbes to the gut
questions.explanation: Antibiotics target bacteria broadly, so they can reduce helpful gut microbes alongside the harmful ones — which is why recovery diets often emphasize fiber.

questions.prompt: Most of the microbes in a healthy human body live in the…
questions.explanation: Trillions of microbes live in the large intestine and colon — it's the densest microbial neighborhood in the body.

questions.prompt: After an antibiotic course, gut populations usually recover faster when meals include…
questions.explanation: Diverse plants feed diverse microbes — that's the most reliable way to rebuild a thriving microbiome after a disturbance.

questions.prompt: Lactobacillus species in fermented foods like yogurt and kefir are part of which kingdom?
questions.explanation: Lactobacillus species are bacteria — they ferment lactose into lactic acid, which gives yogurt its tang.
